test_description: This test case evaluates the generator model's accuracy and technical depth, assuming a perfect retrieval process.
question: [Fill] In an overlay metrology system using polarization-based imaging, the first-layer structure of the overlay target rotates the polarization of one or more _____ diffraction orders relative to zero-order diffraction, enabling isolation of its image by aligning the collection polarizer at _____ degrees relative to the incident illumination polarization, while the second-layer structure maintains the polarization and is isolated by aligning the collection polarizer at _____ degrees.

gold_context_sources.evidence: The first-layer structure 202 includes a first set of periodic features and a second set of periodic features oriented at a 90-degree angle with respect to the first set of periodic features, arranged to rotate a polarization of one or more rotated diffraction orders from the first-layer structure relative to a polarization of zero-order diffraction, wherein the one or more rotated diffraction orders include at least first-order diffraction. The imaging sub-system includes collection polarizers oriented to isolate images by passing polarization-rotated diffraction orders (e.g., at 135 degrees) while blocking zero-order diffraction and vice versa (e.g., at 45 degrees) to isolate the second-layer structure that maintains polarization.

FIG. 4 illustrates zero-order diffraction having the same electric field direction as the incident illumination beam, whereas non-zero diffraction orders are orthogonally polarized to zero-order diffraction. A collection polarizer oriented at 135 degrees blocks zero-order diffraction and isolates rotated non-zero diffraction orders, enabling independent imaging of the first-layer structure.

ground_truth_answer: The correct answer is first-order; 135; 45. Reasoning: The first-layer structure in the overlay target is designed to rotate the polarization of non-zero diffraction orders (at least first-order diffraction) by 90 degrees relative to zero-order diffraction. This allows the collection polarizer to be oriented at 135 degrees relative to the incident illumination polarization to pass only the rotated diffraction orders and block zero-order diffraction, isolating the first-layer structure's image. Conversely, the second-layer structure maintains the incident polarization, so setting the collection polarizer at 45 degrees passes this polarization while blocking the rotated orders, isolating the second-layer structure's image. This polarization-based separation effectively eliminates cross-talk between the layers in overlay metrology.
